---
title: "Supplementary Material"
---

# Supplementary Material {.unnumbered}

Supporting analyses and figures for *"Braided-channel reorganization during storms,
resolved seismically."* The main-text cross-references (e.g. @fig-edge, @fig-traffic)
resolve to the figures collected here. In a journal submission these would be
renumbered S1–S5; for this rendered book they keep their stable labels.

## Text S1 — Anthropogenic-noise screening {.unnumbered}

Candidate lowland stations were screened for traffic/cultural contamination over a
pre-flood week (1–7 December 2025) by the weekday/daytime-versus-night cycle of
4–12 Hz power (`workflows/04_traffic_noise.py`). The glacial-source broadband
stations are river-dominated (traffic index ≈ 1.3–2.6 and weak diurnal structure),
whereas the lowland urban accelerometers carry a strong weekly cycle — UW.TEHA most
severely (traffic index ≈ 59; weekday/weekend ≈ 21; correlation with discharge
$r\approx0$). Contaminated stations are excluded from the transect (drawn hollow on
@fig-map) and from all fits (@fig-traffic).

## Text S2 — Flood-wave lag estimation and validation {.unnumbered}

For a station whose USGS gage is not co-located, gage $Q$ lags the discharge passing
the station by the flood-wave travel time. We estimate a constant $P$–$Q$ lag per
station by cross-correlating the limb-timescale (36-h high-passed) components of
$\log_{10}P$ and $\log_{10}Q$ — the high pass removes the multi-day storm trend that
would otherwise lock the correlation onto spurious large lags — and apply it only
where a real travel time exists (gage $\gtrsim$3 km away; a co-located gage returns
sub-resolution noise). On the Nisqually (gage 13–21 km downstream) the implied
celerities are ≈2 m s⁻¹, physical flood-wave speeds that validate the correction;
the Puyallup cluster (gage 0.2–1.9 km) needs none. A *constant* lag cannot fully
align a multi-kilometre path whose celerity varies with stage, which intrinsically
limits matched-discharge timing at a far-gaged reach (main text §sec-domain).

## Text S3 — Satellite water-threshold ensemble {.unnumbered}

Turbid braided water sits near MNDWI ≈ 0, so the wetted area — and the predicted
geometric baseline drift $\Delta\log_{10}P=\log_{10}(W_\text{post}/W_\text{pre})$ —
is threshold-sensitive. We sweep the MNDWI water threshold over
$\{-0.10,-0.05,0,+0.05,+0.10\}$ and report the median and full range per station
(error bars on @fig-braidsat c). The *sign and rank* of the
inferred channel migration are robust across the ensemble; the calibrated magnitude
is not, and awaits ≈3 m imagery (main text §sec-braided).

## Supplementary Figures {.unnumbered}

![Anthropogenic (traffic) noise screening: median 4–12 Hz power vs hour-of-week over the pre-flood week (weekends shaded). Traffic-polluted stations (red) show a strong weekday/daytime cycle; the river-dominated source stations do not.](figures/figS_traffic_noise.png){#fig-traffic width=85%}

![Per-AR average bedload (×pre-flood median) by station, ordered source→downstream. AR2 dominates at every station and the signal decays strongly downstream. (Supports the bounded time-dependent-bedload hypothesis, main text.)](figures/fig7_bedload_per_AR.png){#fig-blAR width=88%}

![Time-resolved scaling exponent $b(t)$ for the 5–15 Hz band (24 h sliding fit), per station; the turbulence baseline is shaded and the pre-AR/AR1/AR2/AR3 windows marked. The exponent exceeds the baseline during the main pulses — consistent with bedload activation — then relaxes.](figures/fig8_b_of_time.png){#fig-bt width=92%}

![Lower bedload edge (30–50 Hz) from the 100-sps UW broadband stations: flood-vs-quiet spectra and 30–50 Hz vs discharge scaling. UW.LON (Nisqually) shows a tentative high-frequency transport signal; UW.RER (far from its gage) is noise.](figures/fig13_bedload_ch.png){#fig-edge width=95%}

**Spectrogram, and the colocated-infrasound discriminant.** A single flood-vs-quiet
spectrum (@fig-spec) shows *where* in frequency the river signal sits; a continuous
**spectrogram** (@fig-specgram) shows how that band structure evolves through the
event — the view @schmandt2013 used in the Grand Canyon to assign different parts of
the spectrum to different fluvial processes. At the 100-sps Nisqually source station
UW.LON the seismic power across the turbulence band (~1–20 Hz) and the lower bedload
edge (~30–50 Hz) rises and falls with the AR2 hydrograph, and the canonical
clean-gravel bedload band (30–80 Hz) is only partly captured below the 50 Hz Nyquist —
the same coverage limit discussed in the main text. Critically, **the seismic record
alone is spectrally degenerate**: turbulent water flow over the bed and bedload
impacts on the bed radiate into overlapping bands, so a bright band does not by itself
locate the *source* — at the bed, or at the air–water interface. This is the central
ambiguity behind treating our 1–15 Hz signal as turbulence-dominated and fine-gravel
bedload as an upper bound (main text §sec-bedload). Colocated **infrasound** resolves
it on physical grounds: bedload — grains striking the bed — radiates elastic waves into
the ground (seismic) but couples poorly to the atmosphere, whereas free-surface
turbulence (breaking waves, hydraulic jumps, splashing in rapids) at the **air–water
interface** radiates strongly into the air (infrasound) and into air-coupled ground
motion. The fraction of seismic power *coherent with a colocated microbarometer* is
therefore attributable to surface/air–water sources, and the incoherent remainder is
the candidate **bed/in-water** source [@schmandt2013].

**This network does carry infrasound, and we run the test.** Contrary to the earlier
framing that the Cascades stations had no microbarometer, the USGS Cascades Volcano
Observatory (network **CC**) operates **BDF** infrasound arrays colocated with broadband
seismometers along the upper-Puyallup lahar corridor — including at this study's
on-channel anchor **CC.PR03**, which sits on USGS gage 12092000 (Puyallup nr Electron)
and carries colocated 50-sps seismic (BHZ) and 50-sps infrasound (BDF). We use the three
stations with continuous BDF through AR2 — CC.PR03 (1 element), CC.PR04 and CC.STYX
(3-element arrays, a few km up-corridor; CC.PR01/PR02 carried BDF only in 2018–2020).
@fig-infra shows the infrasound is a genuine, energetic field but is **heavily
wind-contaminated**, and its band-integrated 1–20 Hz power does *not* track discharge
consistently (Spearman $r$ from $-0.36$ at PR03 to $+0.56$ at STYX) — so raw infrasound
power is not a clean flow proxy and the discriminant must be coherence, not power.

@fig-coh is that discriminant. Across AR2 the colocated seismic–infrasound coherence
$\gamma^2(f)$ stays at the **noise floor** ($\gamma^2<0.01$, < 1% shared variance) over
0.5–20 Hz at both CC.PR03 and CC.PR04, even at the discharge peak. This is not a dead
sensor: as a positive control, two elements of the CC.PR04 infrasound array are mutually
coherent ($\gamma^2\approx0.2$ at 2–8 Hz), so a real coherent acoustic field is present —
the seismic river band simply is not phase-locked to it. The colocated infrasound
therefore **bounds the air-coupled fraction of the seismic river signal as small**,
supporting a dominantly ground-radiated (bed / in-water-turbulence) origin over coherent
air–water-interface radiation — consistent with treating the 1–15 Hz signal as
turbulence-over-the-bed with bedload as an upper bound (main text §sec-bedload). The
bound is one-sided: spatially-incoherent free-surface turbulence and wind contamination
also lower point coherence, so the result constrains rather than excludes a surface
contribution. The canonical bedload band (30–80 Hz) lies above the 25 Hz infrasound
Nyquist, itself consistent with bedload not radiating into the air.

![Vertical ground-velocity spectrogram at the Nisqually source station UW.LON (100 sps, 50 Hz Nyquist) through the AR2 pulse (7–14 Dec 2025), with the co-located Nisqually-nr-National discharge (USGS 12082500) overlaid (green). Seismic power across the turbulence band (~1–20 Hz) and the lower bedload edge (~30–50 Hz) tracks the two discharge pulses; the canonical bedload band (30–80 Hz) is only partly sampled below Nyquist. Power is relative (raw counts in dB; the broadband response is approximately flat in velocity over this band). The figure underscores that **seismic data alone cannot place the source at the bed (bedload) versus the air–water interface (free-surface turbulence)** — the discrimination the colocated infrasound test (@fig-coh) provides [@schmandt2013].](figures/fig30_spectrogram_seismic.png){#fig-specgram width=98%}

![Colocated CC microbarometer (BDF) infrasound spectrograms through AR2 at three upper-Puyallup stations — CC.PR03 (anchor, on USGS gage 12092000, single element), CC.PR04 and CC.STYX (3-element arrays, elements power-averaged), with the Puyallup-nr-Electron discharge overlaid (green). BDF is 50 sps → 25 Hz Nyquist, so the canonical bedload band (30–80 Hz) is above the infrasound passband entirely. The infrasound is a real, energetic field but is dominated by broadband wind/weather bursts (vertical streaks); band-integrated 1–20 Hz power tracks discharge only weakly and inconsistently across sites (Spearman $r=-0.36$, $+0.36$, $+0.56$ for PR03/PR04/STYX). Raw infrasound power is therefore not a clean river-flow proxy — motivating the response-independent coherence discriminant in @fig-coh.](figures/fig31_infrasound_psd.png){#fig-infra width=98%}

![Colocated seismic–infrasound coherence at the upper-Puyallup arrays — the turbulence-vs-bed discriminant. **(top)** Time-resolved magnitude-squared coherence $\gamma^2(f,t)$ between CC.PR03 colocated seismic (BHZ) and infrasound (BDF) in 1-h windows through AR2, with discharge (pink); coherence stays at the noise floor while discharge spikes. **(bottom)** Flood-peak-averaged $\gamma^2(f)$: both colocated seismic–infrasound pairs (PR03, PR04) hug zero (< 1% shared variance), whereas two elements of the CC.PR04 infrasound array (orange) are mutually coherent ($\gamma^2\approx0.35$ at 2–4 Hz), proving a real coherent acoustic field exists. Coherence is normalised by the auto-spectra, hence independent of instrument response. The seismic river band is not phase-locked to the acoustic field — bounding the air-coupled fraction as small and supporting a ground-radiated (bed/in-water-turbulence) origin [@schmandt2013].](figures/fig32_seis_infrasound_coherence.png){#fig-coh width=98%}

![*(Exploratory.)* Sentinel-1 (SAR) channel-migration series through the December-2025 ARs: each off-channel station's attenuation-weighted wetted illumination relative to the on-channel anchor CC.PR03 (normalised to mid-November), a ratio that cancels per-epoch scene-count and overall-wetness biases. CC.PR01 (braidplain-central) is preferentially illuminated during the flood peak (shaded) — the geometric flow-front response — then relaxes. SAR under-detects shallow braided water at 10 m, so absolute wetted area is not used; only the between-station ratio.](figures/fig20_braid_timeseries.png){#fig-braidsat-ts width=80%}

![Stage–discharge rating curves at the four corridor gages from the December-2025 instantaneous data: discharge $Q$ (log) vs gage height / stage $h$ (linear), with the fitted power law $Q=C\,(h-h_0)^{\beta}$ per gage ($h_0$ = stage of zero flow). The exponent steepens with confinement — $\beta\approx1.6$ at the lowland Puyallup-at-Puyallup mouth and $\approx2.0$ on the Nisqually, up to $\beta\approx5$–7 in the confined, coarse upper-Puyallup reaches (Electron, Orting) where narrow channels are far more stage-sensitive. This is the rating through which a seismic virtual discharge is mapped to stage, $h=h_0+(Q_\text{seis}/C)^{1/\beta}$ (main text §"From discharge to stage"); the linear-axis companion with its time-coloured hysteresis/aggradation diagnostic is @fig-rathyst.](figures/fig15_rating.png){#fig-ratingSI width=85%}

**Stage–discharge hysteresis and what it can (and cannot) say about aggradation.** A
linear stage–discharge view (@fig-rathyst) makes the *non-stationarity* of the gage
ratings legible. Two physically distinct effects open a single-valued rating into a
loop, and only one is even a candidate bed-change diagnostic. (i) *Within-event* loops
(the rising limb tracing a different curve from the falling limb at the same $Q$) are
**dominantly an unsteady-flow / looped-rating (Jones) effect**: the flood-wave
water-surface slope is steeper while the wave is rising, so a given stage conveys more
discharge (equivalently, lower stage at a given $Q$ on the rising limb). Because a
sediment scour-and-fill cycle produces a loop of the *same* sense, the within-event
loop direction by itself cannot distinguish bed scour from deposition
[@williams1989]. (ii) The candidate bed signal is instead the **net drift of the
rating through the AR sequence**: a sustained *rise* in stage at a fixed discharge
means the channel now conveys the same flow at a higher water level. We quantify both
per gage at elevated flow ($Q>$ median): the within-event loop amplitude
(rising−falling stage residual about the mean rating, in $\log_{10}$ units, "dex") and
the net cross-event drift (late−early median shift, and the Spearman $\rho$ of the
stage residual with time). The confined source gages (Puyallup nr Electron, Nisqually
nr National) are essentially single-valued ($\rho\approx0$, drift $\lesssim$ 0.03 dex)
— stable hydraulic controls (a fixed coarse, confined control section). The unconfined
**mid Puyallup-nr-Orting** and **lowland Puyallup-at-Puyallup** gages instead drift
monotonically to higher stage at fixed $Q$ through December ($\rho\approx0.7$; +0.16
and +0.10 dex, i.e. $\sim$25–45 % higher stage at matched discharge by month's end).

We read this drift as **most simply explained by net aggradation** (deposition raising
the bed/control section) in these depositional reaches — but stage–discharge data
*alone* cannot prove it: a rating shift of this sense is also produced by increased
hydraulic roughness, by changing downstream backwater (bar growth, large-wood or debris
jams), by control-section reorganization that is not net bed-raising, or by USGS
rating-shift artifacts [@walling1977], and a seismic/turbidity proxy can likewise
confound a boundary-roughness change with a transport change [@roth2016; @roth2017]. A
strict aggradation claim requires consistency with a sediment-flux divergence (the
Exner balance) and independent bed-elevation evidence, which a point gage does not
provide. What lends the aggradation reading weight here is its **convergence with
independent observations in the same reaches** — the satellite active-channel change
and thread-migration drift (§sec-braided, §sec-reorg; the $+0.2$ cross-AR seismic
baseline drift) and the documented Holocene-to-modern aggradational, supply-rich
sediment regime of the lower Puyallup and Rainier's proglacial basins
[@czuba2011; @beason2022; @anderson2026]. The hysteresis is thus the time-domain counterpart of the
reach-geometry-dependent, event-specific ratings documented for the braided reaches,
and reinforces that a virtual-discharge-to-stage mapping inherits the same
event-specific caveat.

**Ground truth from repeat lidar.** A watershed-scale sediment budget built from
2002–2022 aerial-lidar differencing [@anderson2026; @anderson2025] both supports and
sharpens this reading. It confirms that the **lowland** Puyallup (the lower $\sim$20
valley miles, spanning the Puyallup-at-Puyallup gage) is genuinely aggrading —
$\sim$1.0$\times$10$^6$ m³ of sand-and-gravel deposition over 2004–2020
($\sim$60{,}000 m³ yr⁻¹, continuing a trend back to at least the 1970s) — and
attributes it to a **downstream decrease in channel slope** driving a sediment-flux
divergence (the Exner condition) under an abundant coarse supply. Our lowland rating
drift is thus consistent with an independently documented, ongoing aggradational phase.
The same budget, however, finds the **upper** reaches (near Orting and Electron — our
confined source gages) to be net sediment *sources* whose long-term (60–100 yr)
stage–discharge relations show incision or no trend, and it revises an earlier, larger
1984–2009 upper-reach aggradation estimate downward as a survey-extent artifact. We
therefore read the drift at the upper Puyallup-nr-Orting gage as a **transient,
within-event** rating shift (scour/fill, roughness, backwater), *not* long-term
aggradation, and confine the aggradation interpretation to the lowland reach where the
lidar record independently supports it. Notably, @anderson2026's own bedload proxy — an
empirical bedload–discharge power law applied to these same gages — rises $\sim$40 % in
transport *capacity* over 2004–2020 yet is explicitly cast as capacity, not deposition;
that capacity-vs-delivered-load distinction bounds our seismic proxy in exactly the same
way.

![Stage–discharge ratings on **linear** axes ($h-h_0$ vs $Q$; companion to the log–log @fig-ratingSI), one panel per corridor gage, with every USGS 15-min measurement coloured by time (dark purple = early Dec → yellow = late Dec) and arrows marking the direction of the time-ordered path. A single-valued rating collapses to one curve; departures are hysteresis. **Within-event loops** (rising vs falling limb at matched $Q$) are dominantly an unsteady-flow flood-wave (looped-rating) effect of fixed sense, so loop direction alone cannot separate bed scour from deposition [@williams1989]. The **net rating drift through the AR sequence** is the candidate bed-change signal: a rise in stage at fixed $Q$ is consistent with net **aggradation** (deposition raising the bed/control section); a fall, with net degradation. Inset per panel: within-event loop amplitude, late−early drift, and Spearman $\rho$ of the stage residual with time at elevated flow ($Q>$ median), in $\log_{10}$ units (dex). The confined source gages (Electron, National) are single-valued (stable hydraulic control); the mid (Orting) and lowland (Puyallup) gages drift to higher stage at fixed $Q$ ($\rho\approx0.7$). Only the **lowland** (Puyallup) drift is read as net aggradation — where repeat-lidar differencing independently documents ongoing deposition [@anderson2026]; the upper Orting drift is more likely a transient event-scale shift, since the same study finds the upper reaches to be net-erosional sediment sources over decadal-to-century timescales. Roughness, backwater, and control-section change cannot be excluded from stage–discharge data alone (text below).](figures/fig29_rating_hysteresis_linear.png){#fig-rathyst width=95%}

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
**Supplementary animations.** Two animations accompany the HTML edition: the
space–time virtual-discharge field (@fig-vq) and a repository-only multidisciplinary
loop (SNOTEL + gage + bedload power) at `paper/figures/bedload_animation.gif`.
:::
